meta_title: For Students: analytics setup to Solve can't finish projects (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to analytics setup and overcome can't finish projects. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: Analytics Setup to Overcome Project Stagnation
content.seo_description: Learn how to use analytics to finish your first SaaS project. A beginner-level case study using Antigravity and Claude Code to track user success.
content.answer_block: The root cause of unfinished projects is a lack of visibility. By integrating lightweight analytics, you transform 'guessing' into data-driven development. This shifts your focus from endless feature creep to shipping based on what users actually touch, providing the necessary feedback loop to cross the finish line with confidence.
content.intro:
The graveyard of abandoned GitHub repositories is filled with brilliant ideas that lacked a heartbeat. You start with a burst of inspiration, architecting a masterpiece, only to hit the wall of 'what now?' as the code grows and the momentum fades. This is the silent killer of student projects: building in a void without knowing if your creation actually delivers value.

But what if you could see your users' journey? By implementing a robust analytics layer early, you turn your project into a living organism. Instead of guessing why a feature is ignored, you see the data. This case study shows you how to use Antigravity and Claude Code to instrument your app, giving you the clarity to stop polishing and start shipping.

steps.title: Define Your North Star Metric
steps.description: Before writing code, identify the one action that proves your project works. For a student app, this is usually 'successful signup' or 'first file upload'.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze my project goals and suggest the 3 most important user events to track for a student-built SaaS to measure core utility.
steps.claude_code_command: claude ask 'Review my project README and identify the 3 key events I should track to validate user value.'

steps.title: Initialize the Analytics Provider
steps.description: Setting up the SDK early prevents the 'spaghetti code' trap. We integrate the provider at the root level to ensure global access.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a boilerplate analytics integration for my Next.js project using a lightweight provider that doesn't bloat my bundle size.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Install the chosen analytics SDK and create a hook for event tracking in /lib/analytics.ts'

steps.title: Instrument Critical User Flows
steps.description: Wrap your core functions in tracking events. This turns every click into a data point that justifies your development time.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a wrapper function for my API calls that automatically logs the event, duration, and success status to my analytics provider.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Apply the analytics wrapper to all functions in /components/actions.ts'

steps.title: Configure Real-time Dashboards
steps.description: Visualizing your data is the 'reward' phase. Seeing a chart move proves your code is having an impact in the real world.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a simple dashboard configuration schema that visualizes the conversion rate between 'landing' and 'first action'.
steps.claude_code_command: claude ask 'How do I export my tracked data to a simple dashboard view for my portfolio?'

steps.title: Iterate Based on Data
steps.description: Use the data to kill unused features. If no one clicks it, delete it. This is how you finish projects faster.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review my logs and suggest which feature is currently the highest friction point for my users based on event drop-off rates.
steps.claude_code_command: claude analyze 'Review my recent event logs and report on the highest drop-off point in the user flow.'

pro_tips: Track 'failed' events as aggressively as 'success' events; they reveal where users get stuck.
Never log PII (Personally Identifiable Information) in your events; keep it strictly behavioral.
Use event metadata to store the 'version' of your app to track how updates change behavior.
Automate your analytics reports to Slack or Discord so you get a dopamine hit every time someone uses your app.
If you aren't looking at your dashboard at least once a day, you aren't building a product, you're just writing code.
content.next_steps: Deploy your project to Vercel, share the link in our community, and monitor your first 10 real-world events to validate your hypothesis.
content.conversion_block: Stop building in the dark and start shipping products that people actually use. By instrumenting your workflow, you gain the clarity needed to finish what you start. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
